Received: 29 September 2015 Accepted: 10 February 2016 /Published online: 12 April 2016 Springer-Verlag Berlin Heidelberg 2016

Abstract Purpose The aim of this study is to identify and evaluate the main environmental impacts related to semi-intensive beef cattle production system in the State of Bahia, in Brazil's Northeast. Methods A cradle to gate study was undertaken in a beef cattle production system comprising two stages: calves pro- duction in one farm (farm 1) and cattle production in another farm (farm 2), both located in the central-southern meso-re- gion of Bahia. 5: Cattle production is an important driver for Brazil's economy, the country ranking second worldwide, both in terms of cattle herds, with approximately 212 million head, and in beef pro- duction. Although approximately 84 % of the beef produced in Brazil is destined for the internal market (with a mean consumption of 37 kg/y per inhabitant), since 2004, the coun- try has developed into the largest beef exporter, maintaining trade relations with 180 countries (Brazil 2015). Beef cattle herds are common throughout all of Brazil's territory, whereas the State of Bahia is the main beef producer for the north- eastern region, with the largest herds concentrated in the state's central-southern meso-region.

The above numbers not only reveal the importance of Brazilian cattle production but are also directly related to its impacts on different environmental media, with special refer- ence to biodiversity, soil, air, water and climate. Among the more important impacts are the loss of biodiversity, fertility, and infiltration and water retention capacity of the soil, due to the land use change from natural areas to pasture; eutrophica- tion of surface water resulting from run-off of phosphorus and nitrogen compounds, and the contamination of groundwater by nitrate leaching. There are also the impacts on the climate due to direct emissions of methane from enteric fermentation of cattle and the indirect emissions of nitrous oxide related to fertilizers used in feed production and pasture.

In relation to the contribution to climate change, in 2005, livestock activities were responsible for approximately 12.8 Tg of methane (CH4) emissions from the enteric fermen- tation of ruminants (90 %). Additionally, the N2O emissions amounted to 476 Gg, especially due to animal faeces and urine in the pastures (46 %), and indirect emissions from fertilizers and soil (32 %) (Brazil 2010). According to Ruviaro et al. (2015), the Brazilian beef cattle production is under consider- able pressure from national and international communities concerned with global warming, since it causes over 50 % of the national greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions.

The assessment of interactions of the livestock segment with the environment, such as adverse effects on ecosystems due to environmental impacts, is important due to its complex- ity, its social and economic importance, and its significant demand for natural resources. In recent years, several studies have used the life cycle assessment (LCA) methodology to identify the environmental impacts related to beef cattle pro- duction in Japan (Ogino et al. 2004, 2007), in the USA (Pelletier et al. 2010), in Europe (Nguyen et al. 2010), and Canada (Beauchemin et al. 2010, 2011). In spite of the previ- ously cited relevance of Brazilian cattle production in the in- ternational agribusiness, studies on its impacts are recent. Cederberg et al. (2009, 2011) conducted the first studies fo- cused on Brazilian beef cattle production systems related to Legal Amazon Region. Studies by Ruviaro et al. (2015) and

Dick et al. (2015a, b) were the first developed by Brazilian researchers focused on beef cattle production in Brazil's southern region. This tendency was confirmed by Willers and Rodrigues (2014), who identified these areas as promising for the use of LCA in the country.

In this context, the study of beef cattle production systems in different Brazilian regions can provide an overview of the environmental impacts associated to this activity and on pos- sibilities for their mitigation.
The present study evaluates the main environmental im- pacts related to semi-intensive beef cattle production system in the State of Bahia, in Brazil's Northeast.

10: The Itapetinga micro-region, comprising nine towns, is locat- ed in the centre-south meso-region of Bahia and is one of the most important agricultural and livestock areas due to its ex- cellent soil and climate (Fig. 1). The micro-region has one of the largest cattle populations in Bahia, around 850,000 head (IBGE 2013). According to the Brazilian System of Soil Classification (EMBRAPA 2006), the predominant soils are argisol and chernosol, with a lower share of latosol and alisol (Santana et al. 2002). These soils, especially chernosols, have great potential for farming activities, mostly pasture-based livestock farming. According to Köppen's classification, the climate is Aw, tropical plus a wet season in the summer (Lima and Pinto 2009).
11: In semi-intensive system, the animals are raised in cultivated pastures consisting mainly of species such as Brachiaria and supplemented with mineral salts. For this system, the pasture is divided into smaller pickets, which are used in sequential way to ensure plant recovery due to grazing. Supplementation with salt allows the animal weight gain, even in dry periods, in which the availability of pasture is reduced. The semi- intensive system allows obtaining animals for slaughter in less time than extensive system (Cezar et al. 2005). The product system studied is divided into two stages: calves production in one farm (farm 1) and cattle production in another farm (farm 2).

Calves production: Four bulls and 200 cows reproduce a mean of 100 male and 90 female calves, by artificial insemination as well as by natural breeding. The repro- duction cycle is 9 months, when the calves are born, and an additional 60 to 90 days interval before the next fertil- ization. The calves are weaned after 6 months and the bull
Fig. 1 Centre-south meso-region of Bahia and micro-region of Itapetinga
calves sent to fattening. One part of the heifers is destined to slaughter, whereas the rest of the heifers remain in farm 1 to replace mature breeding cows.
Cattle production: The bull calves are fattened during 20 months. With a life weight of around 480 kg, they are sold to slaughtering.
The farms are characterized as follows:

<list>Farm 1 has a pasture area of 267.9 ha, as well as 14 ponds and 1 creek for livestock watering; the infrastructure con- sists of an access road to the property, silo, corral, stable, administration building and workers housings. 
Farm 2 has a pasture area of 200 ha, 8 ponds and 1 creek for livestock watering; the infrastructure consists of an access road to the property, corral, administration build- ing and workers housings.</list>

The information for the characterization of the production system was retrieved by interviews and questionnaires with the owner or manager and visits to both farms. The collected primary data represented the farm activities during January 2012 to January 2013 and included inputs, technology, infra- structure, and use of natural resources (water, soil, etc.). Accounting documents (sales invoices) related to purchase supplies were used as an additional source of information.
Figure 2 shows the productive system and the correspond- ing unit process considered for modelling, with their respec- tive inputs and outputs.

14: A LCA cradle to gate study was performed according to the standards ISO 14040 (2006) and ISO 14044 (2006). Attributional LCA was used in the present study because it was aimed at the identification of hotspots and at understand- ing of the magnitude of different life cycle stages of the semi- intensive beef cattle production system. This study was defined:
Function: the main economic function was the production of cattle for slaughter.

<list>- Functional unit: defined as 1kg live weight (LW). 
Systems boundary: cradle to gate, including electric- ity, fuels and fertilizers from the background system (related to secondary data) and pasture cultivation, mineral salt production, animal transportation, breeding-rearing and fattening from the foreground system (primary data). 
- Data quality requirements: data and information used in this study were divided into two main categories: primary data and secondary data. The primary data on inputs in</list>
Fig. 2 Analysed beef cattle semi-intensive production system

the stages breeding-rearing, and fattening, and on energy consumption, were collected on a daily basis from the managers of farms 1 and 2, thus providing an elevated level of precision. Secondary data on resource consump- tion, transports and emissions from the production of fuel, pasture cultivation and mineral salt used on the farms were obtained from databases (ecoinvent v.2), reports, studies and empirical and theoretical models.

The software SIMAPRO® 8.0.5 was employed in defin- ing the product system add and correlate data, and per- form the life cycle impact assessment, including an un- certainty analysis by Monte Carlo simulation with 10,000 runs and a 95 % confidence interval. The criteria used to select and define the method and impact categories were the object and purposes of the study, the environmental relevance, as well as the quality and accuracy of the

<list>results, considering the data collected in the Life Cycle Inventory stage (Table 1). Five midpoint impact catego- ries were selected for the study: climate change (CC), terrestrial acidification (TA), freshwater eutrophication (FE), agricultural land occupation (ALO) and fossil de- pletion (FD), using characterization factors from the ReCiPe method (Goedkoop et al. 2009). 
Allocation procedures: mass allocation was used in the multifunctional breeding-rearing unit process. 
- Direct emissions: emissions due to enteric fermentation in ruminants and volatilization from excreta and fertilizers (breeding-rearing and fattening unit process). Such emis- sions were modelled following the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) (Dong et al. 2006), which calculated GHG emissions according to the respec- tive animal categories for the period, per kilogram of LW. The emissions of nitrogen compounds and carbon diox- ide related to fertilizer use in the pastures (pasture culti- vation unit process) were estimated following the models and equations presented by Nemececk and Schnetzer (2011).</list>

17: When compared to calves, the cattle production had the greatest impact in all of the five studied categories, as shown in Table 2.

The process pasture cultivation had expressive impacts in all categories, with 70.05 % (CC), 82.01 % (TA), 76.53 % (FD), 86.14 % (FE) and 100 % (ALO). In CC and TA, the principal elementary flow was the direct emissions due to the use of mineral fertilizers. In FE and FD, the dominant flow was related to the use of phosphate fertilizer. In ALO, the process pasture cultivation was predominant due to the phys- ical area of the farms, which is a characteristic of pasture- based production systems. The processes breeding-rearing and fattening were second in the contribution analysis, with 29.95 and 17.99 %, respectively, for CC and TA, due to the enteric methane emissions and the direct emissions of N2O volatilization from the animal excreta (Fig. 3).

The results of the present study may be compared with other similar studies of beef cattle production systems where one or more equivalent impact categories were considered. The choice of impact categories in this study primarily took into account the Brazilian case studies on extensive beef cattle production systems (Cederberg et al. 2009; Dick et al. 2015b; Ruviaro et al. 2015). In addition, other non-Brazilian studies were also considered, namely the confinement and semi- intensive production systems in Europe (Nguyen et al. 2010) and a confinement production system in Japan for cow-calf (Ogino et al. 2004) and fattening systems (Ogino et al. 2007).

23: In the current study, the processes pasture cultivation (70.0 %), breading-rearing (17.8 %) and fattening (12.2 %) contributed most to the impacts. Direct emissions due to the dominant elementary flow associated to CH4 (100 % for breading-rearing and fattening) from enteric emissions and emissions of N2O from fertilizers (99 % for pasture cultiva- tion) were the most relevant contributors. These results con- firm those found in specific literature (Nguyen et al. 2010; Ogino et al. 2004; Ogino et al. 2007; Dick et al. 2015b; Cederberg et al. 2009; Ruviaro et al. 2015).

Nguyen et al. (2010) studied GHG emissions and empha- sized methane and nitrogenous compounds as the impact- causing factors. Ogino et al. (2004) reported that enteric CH4 emission of cattle in a confinement system for beef fat- tening accounted for 48 % of the total contribution. Feed pro- duction and feed transport accounted for 27 and 14 % of the

total contribution, respectively, mainly due to CO2 emissions in the two stages. Ogino et al. (2007) also reported that the contribution from enteric CH4 emissions of cattle in a cow- calf system accounted for 61.2 % of the total contribution. Feed production and feed transport accounted for 18.4 and 8.3 % of the total contribution, respectively, mainly due to the CO2 emissions in the two processes. Cederberg et al. (2009) identified methane (CH4) from enteric emissions (~76%) and nitrous oxide (N20) (~22 as the most relevant contributions.

Dick et al. (2015b) registered GHG emissions from animals of 84.6 % in the extensive system (ES) and 78.1 % in the improved system (IS), of which 97.27 and 97.35 % in ES and IS, respectively, were due to enteric fermentation. The authors reported that from the GHG emissions from the ani- mals, 63.38 % in ES and 61.65 % in IS stemmed from the animal category "breeding herd." In this study, no relevant difference between breeding-rearing (43.65 %) and fattening (42.57 %) in the CC category could be observed. Ruviaro et al. (2015) studied seven scenarios in beef cattle, ranging between extensive to semi-intensive systems. GHG emissions of the systems were predominantly related to the direct emis- sions of methane (CH4), ranging between 86 and 96 %, which are consistent with all the other previously mentioned studies.
Similar to studies by Ogino et al. (2004, 2007), the present analysis also identified the transport of animals as a contribu- tor, with 7.2 %, due to CO2 emissions.

29: Considering the complete system, the unit process pasture cultivation contributed most (86.14 %) to FE, with the ele- mentary flows related to phosphate and nitrogen compounds in the inputs used in pastures. The phosphate fertilizer run-off accounts for 66.4 % of the elementary flow to FE impact, while urea from N fertilizer for 10.7 %. The impact of the production of phosphate and nitrogen compounds is directly related to energy consumption used in the production of these inputs. Once the inventory data of such fertilizer is based on the European energy matrix, it is believed that the impacts associated could be smaller if the Brazilian energy matrix were considered. Thus, the relevance of the obtained results can be considered since Brazil imports most of the fertilizers, around 70 (ANDA 2016).

In a similar approach, Dick et al. (2015b), who studied beef cattle production in extensive (ES) and improved systems (IS) in southern Brazil, found that the pasture was the main con- tributor for FE, due to nutrient losses. Ogino et al. (2004, 2007) reported for both beef fattening and beef cow-calf sys- items, respectively, that the three main contributors for eutro- phication were NH3 emissions from waste treatment, feed production and animal management. NH3 emissions from an- imal management and waste treatment processes originated from animal wastes. Similar to the present study, NH3 emis- sions from feed production stemmed from the chemical fertil- izers applied to the soil. However, according to Nguyen et al. (2010), the dominant contributor to EP is "direct emissions from fattening" (84-97 with nitrate as the major contrib- utor, followed by ammonia and phosphate.

Confirming the results found by Nguyen et al. (2010) and Dick et al. (2015b), the results of the present study demon- strated that the greatest impacts were due to the land use re- quired during the fattening stage, due to pastures. Dick et al. (2015b) obtained grassland occupation rates equal to or very close to 100 % for the extensive system (ES) and improved system (IS), respectively. Nguyen et al. (2010) identified greater impacts on the ALO related to areas required for the
culture of forages and cereals used in cattle feed for confine- ment production systems.
32: Pasture cultivation (76.53 %) pesticides (14.34 %) and min- eral salt production (9.05 were the processes that contrib- uted most to the impacts in this study. Dominant elementary flows in pasture cultivation were related to phosphate (44.8 %) and nitrogen (12 %) compounds from fertilizers, originating from the energy matrix of their respective supply chain. Similar as observed for FE, the fact that the fertilizer dataset is based on the European energy matrix contributed to the significance of these inputs for the environmental impact. Nevertheless, the results can be considered relevant for this study, because Brazil has to import a significant part of these inputs.

The main contributor for mineral salt production was trans- port (7.77 while the contributing flows for pesticides could not be identified, because the dataset used to model the process did not have any connection to other processes.

The study by Dick et al. (2015b) was the only one in the literature that specifically dealt with FD. However, the results obtained by these authors differed from those in the present study, with 100 % for common salt flow in the extensive system (ES) and a negative contribution of f-111.4 % related to the nitrogen biological flow in pastures.

A study by Nguyen et al. (2010) found that the energy (electricity and diesel) used during the "fattening stage" had the highest contribution (50-58 %) to this category. Ogino et al. (2004, 2007) reported that the production and transport of animal feed were the greatest contributors to energy consumption in both studies. Cederberg et al. (2009) reported that the principal fossil energy consumption was mainly relat- ed to the element flow "production of mineral feed," with marginal contributions from diesel fuel and fertilizers used in pasture management.

34: The cattle production is responsible for the greater part of impacts, compared to the calf production. The direct emis- sions from fertilizers used in the pastures, from enteric emis- sions and from animal wastes in the breeding-rearing and fattening processes were identified as the critical points of the analysed cattle production system.

Measures aiming at the improvement of the feed conver- sion of the animals and at the substitution of synthetic nitrogen fertilizers by natural nitrogen fixation can considerably con- tribute to the reduction of environmental impacts of these production systems. Accordingly, the respective modifica- tions should be considered in future studies on the potential
of these measures to enhance the environmental performance of semi-intensive beef cattle production.

In comparison to other LCA studies, the authors are aware of the differences between the approaches used for the model- ling of the production systems as well as for the definition of the functional unit, reference flows, impact assessment meth- od and impact categories.

According to the literature review, this work was the first to study a semi-intensive cattle beef production system in Brazil's northeaster region. The results of this study will com- plement other similar studies conducted in other regions of the country, following the tendency of growing usage of the LCA methodology in Brazilian agricultural and systems. Accordingly, a contribution to a database for this important national economic activity was intended, as well as to support future studies.
